clinicalStem: A 62-year-old hyperopic woman presents to the emergency department at 11 PM with a 3-hour history of severe right eye pain, headache, nausea, and vomiting. On examination, visual acuity in the right eye is counting fingers. The right eye shows corneal haze, a mid-dilated fixed pupil, and the eyeball feels rock-hard on palpation. Intraocular pressure (IOP) measures 58 mmHg in the right eye. Which of the following is the most appropriate IMMEDIATE management step?
options: Topical timolol 0.5% drops only and arrange ophthalmology review the next morning
Intravenous acetazolamide 500 mg plus topical pilocarpine 2%, topical timolol 0.5%, and topical prednisolone, followed by urgent laser peripheral iridotomy
Emergency vitrectomy to decompress the eye
Oral analgesia and antiemetics while awaiting spontaneous resolution
Topical brimonidine 0.2% alone and discharge with outpatient referral
explanation: Acute angle-closure glaucoma (AACG) is an ocular emergency with IOP often exceeding 50 mmHg, causing rapid optic nerve and corneal damage. Immediate medical treatment aims to lower IOP urgently: IV acetazolamide reduces aqueous production, topical pilocarpine constricts the pupil to pull the iris away from the trabecular meshwork, timolol further reduces aqueous production, and topical steroids reduce inflammation. Definitive treatment is laser peripheral iridotomy (LPI) once the cornea clears, creating an alternative aqueous outflow channel to prevent recurrence.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Topical timolol alone is insufficient for an IOP of 58 mmHg and delaying full treatment overnight risks irreversible optic nerve damage and permanent vision loss.
This is the correct multi-modal emergency approach combining aqueous suppressants and a miotic, followed by definitive laser treatment.
Vitrectomy has no role in acute angle-closure; it is not the appropriate or available emergency intervention for this condition.
AACG does not resolve spontaneously at this IOP level; every hour of delay compounds irreversible optic nerve damage.
Brimonidine alone provides minimal IOP reduction and is wholly inadequate for an IOP of 58 mmHg; outpatient referral is dangerously delayed.
clinicalPearl: Acute angle-closure glaucoma requires simultaneous multi-drug IOP lowering (IV acetazolamide + topical pilocarpine + beta-blocker) as a true ocular emergency; definitive treatment is laser peripheral iridotomy.
keyClue: IOP 58 mmHg, mid-dilated fixed pupil, corneal haze, rock-hard eye in a hyperopic woman — classic acute angle-closure.
commonExamTrap: Mistaking the headache and vomiting for migraine or a neurological emergency, leading to CT head rather than urgent IOP measurement and ophthalmic treatment.
missedConcept: The student has missed that AACG is an ophthalmic emergency requiring immediate combined medical therapy before definitive laser iridotomy, not observation or monotherapy.
redFlagReason: IOP of 58 mmHg causes irreversible optic nerve damage within hours; delay in treatment leads to permanent blindness — this is a genuine ocular emergency.

clinicalStem: A 72-year-old man with hypertension and atrial fibrillation presents with sudden, painless, complete loss of vision in his left eye that began 90 minutes ago. Visual acuity is no light perception in the left eye. Fundoscopy reveals a pale, opaque retina with a cherry-red spot at the fovea, and the arterioles appear attenuated. The contralateral eye is normal. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Reassure the patient that vision often recovers spontaneously within 24 hours and arrange outpatient ophthalmology review
Start systemic anticoagulation with heparin as the first-line treatment to dissolve the occlusion
Immediate ocular massage, IOP-lowering agents (topical timolol + IV acetazolamide), and urgent referral for possible thrombolysis protocol
Arrange urgent MRI brain only, as this is most likely a stroke with amaurosis fugax
Apply a pad and patch to rest the eye and prescribe high-dose oral steroids
explanation: Central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO) is an ocular stroke equivalent requiring treatment within the first 4-6 hours (ideally within 90 minutes) to salvage vision. Immediate measures include ocular massage to dislodge an embolus distally, IOP-lowering (acetazolamide IV, topical beta-blocker) to improve perfusion pressure gradient, and carbogen inhalation or administration of vasodilators where available. Emergent intra-arterial thrombolysis via neurointerventional radiology is being evaluated at specialist centres within the time window. Systemic cardiac and stroke workup (ECG for AF, carotid Doppler) is simultaneously essential.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Vision rarely recovers spontaneously in true CRAO; irreversible retinal ischaemia begins within 90 minutes, making watchful waiting unacceptable.
Systemic anticoagulation with heparin is not first-line for CRAO as most cases are embolic, not thrombotic; it does not actively restore perfusion and carries bleeding risk.
This is correct — immediate mechanical (massage) and pharmacological (IOP reduction) steps combined with urgent specialist referral for time-sensitive thrombolysis protocols.
This presentation is complete painless monocular blindness with a cherry-red spot — CRAO, not amaurosis fugax (which is transient); MRI alone without ocular treatment wastes the therapeutic window.
Patching and steroids have no role in CRAO; steroids are used for giant cell arteritis-related AION, not embolic CRAO.
clinicalPearl: CRAO is an ocular stroke — cherry-red spot on a pale retina means act within 90 minutes; ocular massage and IOP reduction aim to improve retinal perfusion pressure while organising specialist thrombolysis.
keyClue: Pale retina with cherry-red spot at the fovea and attenuated arterioles in sudden painless monocular blindness = CRAO.
commonExamTrap: Confusing CRAO with amaurosis fugax (transient) or with CRVO (which causes dilated tortuous veins and haemorrhages, not a pale retina).
missedConcept: The student has missed that CRAO is a time-critical ocular emergency and that immediate IOP-lowering plus ocular massage are essential first steps while organising thrombolysis workup.
redFlagReason: CRAO causes irreversible retinal infarction within 90 minutes; delay in management leads to permanent profound visual loss and misses a narrow therapeutic window.

topic: Giant cell arteritis (temporal arteritis) with anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy
clinicalStem: A 78-year-old woman presents with sudden painless loss of vision in her right eye since waking this morning. She reports a 3-week history of right-sided headache, jaw pain on chewing, and scalp tenderness when brushing her hair. Visual acuity is 6/60 right eye. Fundoscopy shows a swollen, pale optic disc on the right. ESR is 98 mm/hr and CRP is 45 mg/L. What is the most critical immediate management step?
options: Arrange temporal artery biopsy first and start steroids only after histological confirmation
Start high-dose IV methylprednisolone (500 mg–1 g daily) immediately, before biopsy, to prevent bilateral blindness
Refer urgently to neurology for MRI brain to exclude stroke
Start aspirin 300 mg and arrange outpatient rheumatology referral within one week
Prescribe topical NSAIDs for the optic disc swelling and arrange urgent ESR repeat
explanation: Giant cell arteritis (GCA) presenting with visual loss is a medical emergency. High-dose corticosteroids must be started immediately — before temporal artery biopsy — to prevent involvement of the fellow eye, which can occur within days. IV methylprednisolone 500 mg–1 g daily for 3 days is recommended when vision is already affected. Biopsy remains the diagnostic gold standard but should not delay treatment, as steroids do not affect biopsy results for up to 2 weeks. Once the patient is stable, transition to oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Waiting for biopsy confirmation before starting steroids is dangerous; the fellow eye can lose vision within 24-48 hours without treatment — biopsy must not delay steroids.
This is correct — immediate high-dose IV steroids are the priority to protect the fellow eye, with biopsy arranged within 1-2 weeks while treatment continues.
MRI brain is not the first priority; the clinical picture of jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, elevated ESR/CRP with optic disc swelling points firmly to GCA requiring urgent steroid treatment.
Aspirin alone and delayed outpatient review are wholly inadequate; GCA with visual loss requires same-day hospital admission and IV steroids.
Topical NSAIDs have no role in GCA; this is a systemic large-vessel vasculitis requiring systemic immunosuppression.
clinicalPearl: In GCA with visual loss, start high-dose IV corticosteroids immediately — do NOT wait for biopsy; the fellow eye is at imminent risk of irreversible blindness.
keyClue: Jaw claudication + scalp tenderness + sudden visual loss + elevated ESR/CRP in an elderly woman = GCA with AION until proven otherwise.
commonExamTrap: Delaying steroids until biopsy results return — biopsy can be done within 1-2 weeks while steroids are running and will still be diagnostic.
missedConcept: The student has missed that bilateral blindness in GCA is preventable only by immediate high-dose steroids before histological confirmation.
redFlagReason: GCA can cause bilateral irreversible blindness within days if steroids are delayed; this is a same-day ophthalmic and medical emergency.

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old myopic man presents with a 2-day history of a sudden onset of floaters and flashing lights in his right eye. Over the last 12 hours he has noticed a dark shadow advancing from the inferior visual field toward the centre of his vision. Visual acuity is 6/9 right eye. Fundoscopy reveals a bullous retinal detachment in the inferior and temporal retina, but the macula appears flat and attached. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Reassure and review in 2 weeks as the macula is still attached
Prescribe topical steroids and broad-spectrum antibiotics for presumed uveitis
Same-day emergency surgical repair (scleral buckle or vitrectomy) to prevent macular involvement
Arrange laser retinopexy in an outpatient clinic within 4 weeks
Order an urgent MRI orbit to characterise the detachment before any intervention
explanation: A rhegmatogenous retinal detachment (RRD) with macula-on (macula still attached) is a surgical emergency because macula involvement transforms the prognosis from good to guarded. Once the macula detaches, even successful surgical reattachment rarely restores pre-morbid central visual acuity. Same-day or next-morning surgical repair — via scleral buckle, vitrectomy, or pneumatic retinopexy — is mandated when the macula is threatened. Delaying beyond 24 hours significantly increases the risk of macular detachment and permanent visual loss.
wrongAnswerExplanations: A 2-week wait is completely inappropriate; the advancing shadow means the detachment is progressing toward the macula and same-day surgery is required.
This presentation is not uveitis — a bullous retinal detachment with floaters and shadow in a myope requires surgical intervention, not topical medication.
This is correct — macula-on RRD is an ophthalmic surgical emergency; same-day repair preserves central vision.
Outpatient laser retinopexy may be appropriate for retinal tears without detachment, not a bullous RRD threatening the macula.
MRI is not part of the workup for a clinically evident RRD; it delays surgical treatment unnecessarily.
clinicalPearl: Macula-on rhegmatogenous retinal detachment is a surgical emergency — operate the same day to preserve central vision before the macula detaches.
keyClue: Bullous inferior RRD advancing toward centre with flat macula = macula-on RRD requiring same-day surgery.
commonExamTrap: Falsely reassured by the preserved visual acuity (6/9) and intact macula, leading to a delayed surgical plan.
missedConcept: The student has missed that macula-on RRD is time-critical; once the macula detaches, visual prognosis degrades significantly even with successful repair.
redFlagReason: Macula-on RRD can progress to macula-off within hours to days; delay in surgery results in permanent central visual loss.

clinicalStem: A 9-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with a 2-day history of right eye swelling, redness, and fever. On examination, temperature is 38.9°C, there is proptosis of the right eye, restricted and painful extraocular movements in all directions, and reduced visual acuity of 6/24 on the right. There is no afferent pupillary defect. CT orbit with contrast shows preseptal and postseptal fat stranding with a subperiosteal abscess along the medial orbital wall. Blood cultures are taken. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate and review in 48 hours as an outpatient
Topical antibiotic drops and warm compresses with outpatient ENT referral within one week
IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., IV co-amoxiclav or IV cefuroxime plus metronidazole) with same-day surgical drainage of the subperiosteal abscess
Intravenous steroids alone to reduce orbital inflammation and scheduled MRI in 24 hours
IV antifungal therapy for presumed mucormycosis and arrange biopsy
explanation: Orbital cellulitis with a subperiosteal abscess (postseptal cellulitis) in a child is a sight- and life-threatening emergency. Postseptal involvement is confirmed by proptosis, restricted painful eye movements, and CT evidence of abscess. Management requires hospital admission, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics covering staphylococci, streptococci, and anaerobes (e.g., IV co-amoxiclav or cefuroxime + metronidazole), and same-day surgical drainage of any subperiosteal abscess. Visual acuity and pupil responses must be monitored closely for optic nerve compromise; an afferent pupillary defect signals worsening compressive optic neuropathy requiring immediate drainage.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Oral antibiotics are insufficient for postseptal cellulitis with an abscess; this requires IV antibiotics and surgical drainage to prevent optic nerve damage and intracranial spread.
Topical drops do not penetrate the orbit; outpatient ENT in one week is dangerously delayed for a postseptal abscess.
This is correct — IV antibiotics plus same-day surgical drainage of the subperiosteal abscess are the standard of care for orbital cellulitis with abscess in a child.
Steroids alone without antibiotics and drainage would worsen the infection; orbital decompression requires surgery, not steroids.

Mucormycosis is a consideration in immunocompromised or diabetic patients with a different clinical picture; a healthy child with sinusitis-related orbital abscess needs bacterial coverage and surgical drainage.
clinicalPearl: Orbital cellulitis with subperiosteal abscess requires IV antibiotics PLUS surgical drainage; monitor visual acuity and pupils — a new APD signals compressive optic neuropathy and demands immediate surgery.
keyClue: Postseptal inflammation (proptosis + restricted painful EOM) with CT-confirmed subperiosteal abscess in a febrile child = orbital cellulitis requiring IV antibiotics and drainage.
commonExamTrap: Confusing preseptal (periorbital) cellulitis — which can be managed with oral antibiotics — with postseptal/orbital cellulitis, which requires IV antibiotics and often surgery.
missedConcept: The student has missed the distinction between preseptal and postseptal cellulitis and the need for surgical drainage of a confirmed abscess.
redFlagReason: Orbital cellulitis with abscess can cause compressive optic neuropathy and intracranial spread (cavernous sinus thrombosis, meningitis) — it is a paediatric surgical emergency.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old man with known HLA-B27-positive ankylosing spondylitis presents with a 3-day history of pain, photophobia, and redness of his left eye. Visual acuity is 6/12 on the left. Slit-lamp examination shows circumlimbal (ciliary) flush, 3+ cells and flare in the anterior chamber, and keratic precipitates on the corneal endothelium. The pupil is slightly irregular. IOP is 16 mmHg. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment?
options: Topical antibiotics (chloramphenicol) and warm compresses for presumed bacterial conjunctivitis
Oral prednisolone 60 mg daily as first-line monotherapy without topical treatment
Topical corticosteroids (e.g., prednisolone acetate 1% hourly) plus a cycloplegic agent (e.g., cyclopentolate 1% TDS)
Topical antiviral drops (acyclovir) for herpetic anterior uveitis
Topical beta-blocker monotherapy to control the elevated IOP
explanation: Anterior uveitis is treated with topical corticosteroids to suppress intraocular inflammation and a cycloplegic/mydriatic agent (cyclopentolate or atropine) for two purposes: relieving painful ciliary muscle spasm and dilating the pupil to prevent posterior synechiae (adhesions between iris and lens). Prednisolone acetate 1% is the standard topical steroid, dosed frequently (every 1-2 hours initially), then tapered. Cyclopentolate 1% three times daily is a common cycloplegic. Systemic steroids are generally not first-line for anterior uveitis unless severe bilateral disease.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Topical antibiotics treat bacterial infections, not uveitis; ciliary flush, cells/flare, and keratic precipitates are hallmarks of intraocular inflammation, not surface infection.
Systemic oral steroids are not first-line for anterior uveitis; topical therapy is the mainstay, and high-dose oral prednisolone has significant systemic side effects.
This is correct — topical corticosteroid + cycloplegic is the first-line treatment for acute anterior uveitis.

Topical antivirals are appropriate for herpetic keratitis but not for non-herpetic HLA-B27-associated anterior uveitis; this patient's association with ankylosing spondylitis points to an HLA-B27 seronegative spondyloarthropathy aetiology.
The IOP is normal (16 mmHg) and does not need treatment; the primary pathology is inflammation, which requires steroids and cycloplegia.
clinicalPearl: Acute anterior uveitis is treated with topical corticosteroids (suppress inflammation) plus cycloplegics (relieve spasm and prevent posterior synechiae) — never with antibiotics or antivirals unless a specific infective cause is confirmed.
keyClue: HLA-B27-positive patient with ciliary flush, cells/flare, and keratic precipitates = anterior uveitis requiring topical steroids and cycloplegics.
commonExamTrap: Treating the red eye with topical antibiotics as 'conjunctivitis' when the slit-lamp findings (KPs, cells/flare, ciliary flush) clearly point to anterior uveitis.
missedConcept: The student has missed the role of cycloplegics in anterior uveitis: they relieve ciliary spasm AND prevent posterior synechiae formation.

clinicalStem: A 55-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes mellitus for 14 years presents for her annual retinal review. She reports mild blurring of central vision in the right eye for 3 months. Visual acuity is 6/18 right eye and 6/6 left eye. Dilated fundoscopy shows dot and blot haemorrhages and hard exudates within one disc diameter of the fovea. Optical coherence tomography (OCT) confirms central subfield thickness of 380 micrometres in the right eye (normal <250 µm), with intraretinal fluid. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for the right eye?
options: Intravitreal anti-VEGF injection (e.g., ranibizumab or aflibercept) as first-line treatment
Pan-retinal laser photocoagulation to treat the centre-involving diabetic macular oedema
Topical NSAIDs to reduce macular oedema
Oral carbonic anhydrase inhibitor (acetazolamide) to reduce macular thickness
Optimise glycaemic and blood pressure control only, with repeat OCT in 6 months
explanation: Centre-involving diabetic macular oedema (CI-DMO) with reduced visual acuity is the leading cause of vision loss in diabetic retinopathy and is primarily treated with intravitreal anti-VEGF agents (ranibizumab, aflibercept, or bevacizumab) as per current NICE guidelines and international consensus. Anti-VEGF injections have been shown to be superior to macular laser for CI-DMO with vision impairment. Macular laser is now reserved for non-centre-involving DMO or as an adjunct. Systemic optimisation of HbA1c and blood pressure is important but insufficient as sole treatment when vision is already impaired.
wrongAnswerExplanations: This is correct — intravitreal anti-VEGF is first-line for centre-involving DMO with visual acuity loss.
Pan-retinal photocoagulation (PRP) is used for proliferative diabetic retinopathy, not for centre-involving macular oedema; focal/grid macular laser is occasionally used for non-CI-DMO.
Topical NSAIDs do not adequately penetrate to the macula and are not evidence-based for DMO.
Oral acetazolamide has no established role in diabetic macular oedema management.
Systemic optimisation alone is insufficient when CI-DMO has already reduced visual acuity to 6/18; intravitreal treatment is needed promptly to preserve and restore vision.
clinicalPearl: Centre-involving diabetic macular oedema with visual acuity loss is treated with intravitreal anti-VEGF injections as first-line — not macular laser, which is now reserved for non-centre-involving DMO.
keyClue: Central subfield thickness 380 µm with intraretinal fluid and VA 6/18 in a diabetic = centre-involving DMO requiring anti-VEGF.
commonExamTrap: Choosing pan-retinal laser (for PDR) instead of anti-VEGF (for CI-DMO); or choosing macular laser instead of anti-VEGF for centre-involving disease with VA loss.
missedConcept: The student has missed the paradigm shift: anti-VEGF has replaced macular laser as first-line for centre-involving DMO with vision impairment.

clinicalStem: A 4-year-old girl is referred by her paediatrician after failing a vision screening test. On examination, visual acuity is 6/6 in the right eye and 6/36 in the left eye. Cover test reveals a constant left esotropia. Cycloplegic refraction shows +3.00 DS in both eyes (no significant anisometropia). Anterior and posterior segment examination is normal bilaterally. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Immediate strabismus surgery to correct the esotropia before prescribing glasses
Prescribe full hypermetropic correction glasses and reassess in 3 months; if amblyopia persists, add patching of the right (dominant) eye
Patch the left (amblyopic) eye to stimulate the right eye
Refer for botulinum toxin injection into the left medial rectus as first-line treatment
Reassure parents that vision will equalise on its own as the child grows
explanation: In an accommodative esotropia with bilateral hypermetropia, the first step is to prescribe the full cycloplegic refractive correction (glasses). This alone may reduce or eliminate the esotropia and allow vision development. Amblyopia treatment (patching of the fellow/dominant eye) is added if the visual deficit persists after adequate optical correction. Surgery is considered only once the refractive component has been optimised and any residual angle quantified. Patching the amblyopic eye (left) rather than the dominant eye would be counterproductive — the fellow (right/dominant) eye is patched to force use of the amblyopic eye.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Surgery before optical correction is incorrect; accommodative esotropia often resolves or reduces substantially with glasses alone, so surgery without a refractive trial is premature.
This is correct — prescribe full hypermetropic correction first, then add patching of the dominant right eye if amblyopia persists after spectacle wear.
Patching the amblyopic (left) eye is the wrong eye — the dominant (right) eye should be patched to force the brain to use the amblyopic left eye.
Botulinum toxin injection has a limited and specialist role; it is not first-line ahead of optical correction.
Amblyopia does not spontaneously resolve; the critical period for amblyopia treatment is before age 7-8, and inaction risks permanent visual loss in the left eye.
clinicalPearl: In accommodative esotropia with amblyopia, always prescribe full refractive correction first — it may resolve both the squint and the amblyopia; patching (of the DOMINANT eye) is added if amblyopia persists.
keyClue: Bilateral +3.00 DS hypermetropia with accommodative esotropia and amblyopia in a 4-year-old = glasses-first approach before patching or surgery.
commonExamTrap: Patching the amblyopic (weaker) eye instead of the dominant eye, or proceeding to strabismus surgery before optimising the refractive error.
missedConcept: The student has missed the correct eye to patch (the dominant eye is patched to force use of the amblyopic eye) and the importance of optical correction as the initial step in accommodative esotropia.

clinicalStem: A 35-year-old woman presents with a 4-day history of swelling, redness, and tenderness over the medial canthus of her right eye, below the medial canthal tendon. The area is fluctuant on palpation. She has a fever of 38.2°C and reports a history of recurrent right-sided epiphora (watering eye) over the past year. Visual acuity is normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Topical antibiotic eye drops alone and review in one week
Immediate dacryocystorhinostomy (DCR) as the definitive first-line treatment
Systemic antibiotics (oral or IV depending on severity) plus warm compresses; arrange DCR after the acute episode resolves
Incision and drainage of the abscess immediately without antibiotic coverage
Prescribe oral antihistamines for presumed allergic dacryocystitis
explanation: Acute dacryocystitis is an infection of the lacrimal sac, typically caused by Staphylococcus aureus or Streptococcus species secondary to nasolacrimal duct obstruction (NLDO). Management involves systemic antibiotics — oral (e.g., amoxicillin-clavulanate) for mild cases or IV (e.g., co-amoxiclav) for severe/fluctuant cases — plus warm compresses. If a frank abscess forms, incision and drainage may be needed alongside antibiotics. Definitive treatment of the underlying NLDO to prevent recurrence is dacryocystorhinostomy (DCR), but this is an elective procedure performed once the acute infection has fully resolved. Topical drops alone do not achieve adequate tissue penetration.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Topical drops alone do not penetrate the lacrimal sac adequately; systemic antibiotics are required for acute dacryocystitis.
DCR is the definitive treatment for recurrent dacryocystitis but must not be performed during active infection; it is an elective procedure after the acute episode resolves.
This is correct — systemic antibiotics treat the acute infection, and DCR is planned electively afterwards to address the underlying NLDO.
Incision and drainage alone without antibiotics is incomplete treatment; antibiotics are required alongside surgical drainage to control systemic spread.
Dacryocystitis is infective/obstructive, not allergic; antihistamines have no role.
clinicalPearl: Acute dacryocystitis = systemic antibiotics (not topical) plus warm compresses acutely; DCR is performed electively once the infection clears to prevent recurrence.
keyClue: Fluctuant, tender medial canthal swelling below the medial canthal tendon with a history of epiphora = acute dacryocystitis from NLDO.
commonExamTrap: Prescribing topical antibiotic drops alone, which do not achieve adequate concentrations in the lacrimal sac, or performing DCR during the acute infective phase.
missedConcept: The student has missed that DCR (the definitive surgery) must be deferred until the acute infection resolves; systemic antibiotics are the bridge treatment.

topic: Normal tension glaucoma vs elevated IOP glaucoma
clinicalStem: A 58-year-old man presents to the ophthalmology clinic. Automated visual field testing shows bilateral superior arcuate scotomas. IOP is 24 mmHg in the right eye and 26 mmHg in the left eye on two separate visits. Central corneal thickness is normal (545 µm). Optic disc examination reveals vertical cup-to-disc ratio of 0.7 bilaterally with inferior neuroretinal rim thinning. Anterior chamber angles are open on gonioscopy. Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Prostaglandin analogue eye drops (e.g., latanoprost 0.005% once nightly) to lower IOP
Pan-retinal laser photocoagulation to halt glaucomatous progression
Systemic carbonic anhydrase inhibitor (oral acetazolamide 250 mg QDS) as the preferred first-line agent
Bilateral trabeculectomy as definitive first-line surgical treatment
Observation only since IOP is within the 'normal' range (< 30 mmHg) and no treatment is needed
explanation: Primary open-angle glaucoma (POAG) is managed by reducing IOP to a 'target pressure' that halts or slows progression. First-line topical therapy per NICE guidelines is a prostaglandin analogue (PGA) such as latanoprost 0.005% once nightly, as PGAs achieve the greatest IOP reduction (25-33%) of any topical class with once-daily dosing and good tolerability. Topical beta-blockers (e.g., timolol) are an alternative first-line option. Oral acetazolamide is reserved for short-term use when topical agents are insufficient or contraindicated. Surgery (trabeculectomy or MIGS) is reserved for advanced disease or failure of medical therapy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: This is correct — prostaglandin analogues are first-line topical therapy for POAG, providing the greatest IOP reduction with once-daily dosing.
Pan-retinal photocoagulation (PRP) treats proliferative diabetic retinopathy and ischaemic retinal conditions; it has no role in POAG.
Oral acetazolamide has significant systemic side effects (renal stones, paraesthesia, electrolyte disturbance) and is reserved for short-term use or acute glaucoma, not long-term POAG management.
Trabeculectomy is effective but invasive; it is not first-line for newly diagnosed POAG — medical therapy is trialled first.
An IOP of 24-26 mmHg with documented disc damage and visual field loss is confirmed POAG requiring treatment; observation would allow progressive and irreversible optic nerve damage.
clinicalPearl: Prostaglandin analogues (e.g., latanoprost) are first-line for POAG — greatest IOP reduction, once-daily, well-tolerated; systemic acetazolamide is reserved for acute or refractory cases.
keyClue: Open angles on gonioscopy + raised IOP + vertical cup-disc ratio 0.7 with arcuate scotomas = POAG requiring topical IOP-lowering therapy.
commonExamTrap: Choosing oral acetazolamide as first-line, which is inappropriate for long-term POAG management due to systemic side effects.
missedConcept: The student has missed that prostaglandin analogues are the preferred first-line agents for POAG, not systemic carbonic anhydrase inhibitors.

topic: Posterior communicating artery aneurysm and CN III palsy
clinicalStem: A 50-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a sudden severe headache she describes as 'the worst of her life,' accompanied by vomiting. On examination, there is complete ptosis of the right eyelid. When the eyelid is manually elevated, the right eye is deviated 'down and out,' and the right pupil is 7 mm and non-reactive to light. Left eye and neurological examination are otherwise normal. CT head is reported as normal. What is the most critical immediate next step?
options: Reassure and discharge with analgesia as the CT head is normal and pupil-involving CN III palsy can be managed as outpatient
Arrange an elective MRI orbit in 2 weeks for cranial nerve imaging
Urgent lumbar puncture to exclude subarachnoid haemorrhage, followed by CT angiography or DSA to identify a posterior communicating artery aneurysm
Start IV methylprednisolone for presumed giant cell arteritis-related cranial nerve palsy
Refer urgently to ophthalmology for botulinum toxin injection to correct the diplopia
explanation: A pupil-involving complete CN III palsy ('down and out' eye with complete ptosis and a dilated unreactive pupil) in the context of a thunderclap headache is a posterior communicating artery (PCoA) aneurysm until proven otherwise. CT head has up to 5-10% false negative rate for SAH in early hours; a normal CT mandates lumbar puncture (LP) to detect xanthochromia or blood. CT angiography (CTA) or digital subtraction angiography (DSA) then identifies the aneurysm for urgent neurosurgical or endovascular coiling. Pupillary involvement distinguishes compressive (surgical) from ischaemic (medical/diabetic) CN III palsy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Discharging with a normal CT and a pupil-involving CN III palsy after a thunderclap headache is a potentially fatal error; LP is mandatory to exclude SAH as CT can be falsely negative.
An elective outpatient MRI in 2 weeks is wholly inappropriate for a potential PCoA aneurysm with thunderclap headache — delayed diagnosis risks catastrophic aneurysm rupture.
This is correct — normal CT does not exclude SAH; LP for xanthochromia followed by CTA/DSA is mandatory when PCoA aneurysm is suspected.
IV methylprednisolone treats inflammatory/vasculitic cranial nerve palsies; GCA does not typically present with thunderclap headache and pupil-involving CN III palsy in this age group.
Botulinum toxin manages cosmetic diplopia in stable microvascular palsies; it has no role in an acute compressive CN III palsy from a suspected aneurysm, which is a neurosurgical emergency.
clinicalPearl: Pupil-involving CN III palsy + thunderclap headache = PCoA aneurysm until proven otherwise — normal CT does NOT exclude SAH; LP for xanthochromia is mandatory, followed by CTA/DSA.
keyClue: 'Down and out' eye with a dilated non-reactive pupil and the worst headache of her life = PCoA aneurysm compressing CN III — neurosurgical emergency.
commonExamTrap: Being falsely reassured by a normal CT head and failing to perform LP; or confusing this with a painless pupil-sparing CN III palsy (ischaemic/diabetic), which does NOT need emergency angiography.
missedConcept: The student has missed the critical distinction: pupil involvement in CN III palsy indicates extrinsic compression (aneurysm) and mandates emergency vascular imaging after LP.
redFlagReason: Unruptured PCoA aneurysm with sentinel headache can rupture causing massive SAH; failure to diagnose and treat urgently is potentially fatal.

clinicalStem: A 74-year-old woman with known dry AMD presents with a 5-day history of new distortion (metamorphopsia) and reduced central vision in her right eye. Best-corrected visual acuity has dropped from 6/12 to 6/36 over 5 days. OCT macula shows subretinal fluid and a type 2 (classic) choroidal neovascular membrane beneath the fovea. Fluorescein angiography confirms active leakage. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Continue monitoring with Amsler grid at home and arrange OCT repeat in 3 months
Urgent intravitreal anti-VEGF injection (e.g., ranibizumab, aflibercept, or bevacizumab) within days
Macular laser photocoagulation directly over the subfoveal choroidal neovascular membrane
Oral antioxidant vitamins (AREDS formula) as first-line treatment for wet AMD
Intravitreal triamcinolone acetonide injection as first-line monotherapy
explanation: Newly diagnosed wet (neovascular) AMD with active choroidal neovascularisation (CNV) and rapid vision loss requires urgent intravitreal anti-VEGF therapy. Anti-VEGF agents (ranibizumab, aflibercept, brolucizumab, or bevacizumab) are the gold-standard first-line treatment, suppressing VEGF-driven neovascularisation and exudation. Treatment should be initiated within days of diagnosis to prevent further photoreceptor damage. Laser photocoagulation over subfoveal CNV would destroy central foveal photoreceptors, causing immediate scotoma. AREDS vitamins reduce progression of dry AMD to wet AMD but do not treat active wet AMD.
wrongAnswerExplanations: A 3-month delay for active wet AMD with vision dropping from 6/12 to 6/36 in 5 days is clinically harmful; anti-VEGF must be started urgently within days.
This is correct — urgent intravitreal anti-VEGF is the evidence-based first-line treatment for neovascular AMD with active CNV.
Subfoveal laser photocoagulation causes immediate central scotoma by burning foveal photoreceptors; it is contraindicated for subfoveal CNV.
AREDS vitamins (zinc, vitamins C and E, lutein, zeaxanthin) reduce risk of dry AMD progressing to wet AMD; they have no therapeutic effect on active wet AMD.
Intravitreal triamcinolone is used adjunctively in some retinal conditions but is not first-line monotherapy for wet AMD; anti-VEGF is superior in all major trials.
clinicalPearl: Wet AMD with active subfoveal CNV requires urgent intravitreal anti-VEGF within days — not weeks; AREDS vitamins are for dry AMD prevention, not treatment of active wet AMD.
keyClue: Sudden metamorphopsia + drop in VA + subretinal fluid + type 2 CNV on OCT = active wet AMD requiring urgent anti-VEGF.
commonExamTrap: Choosing AREDS vitamins (which are preventive for dry AMD) or macular laser (which is contraindicated over the subfovea) instead of anti-VEGF for wet AMD.
missedConcept: The student has missed that AREDS formula treats dry AMD risk reduction, not active wet AMD, and that anti-VEGF must be started urgently to limit photoreceptor loss.
